topic: Mamdani Arrest Netanyahu Enforce Warrant Mayor

left.title: Mamdani says he can’t arrest Netanyahu as mayor and urges US to enforce ICC warrant

left-center.title: Mamdani: NYC Doesn’t Have ‘Legal Authority’ To Enforce ICC Arrest Warrant Against Netanyahu

farLeftText: Zohran Mamdani, the New York City mayor, doubled down on his support for the international criminal court (ICC) on Wednesday and called on federal authorities to execute the court’s warrant for Benjamin Netanyahu’s arrest should he visit the city in the fall. “I call on the federal government to join the international criminal court and to execute both this warrant as well as any warrant the international criminal court holds,” Mamdani told reporters at a press conference on Wednesday. Those remarks came less than 24 hours after Mamdani called the Israeli prime minister a “war criminal” who is “not welcome in New York City” in a video statement posted on social media. In the statement, the mayor acknowledged he had no legal authority to direct New York police to arrest Netanyahu should he attend the United Nations general assembly in September. Mamdani had previously said he was “in conversation” with city lawyers to determine whether his administration could arrest Netanyahu, as he had pledged to do during his mayoral campaign last year. On Wednesday, Mamdani rejected arguments from critics that Israel’s war crimes in Gaza have nothing to do with New York City. Israel’s actions in Gaza, he said, had “offended the conscience, the morals, the mere humanity of so many New Yorkers”. Mamdani’s statements about Netanyahu were remarkable for a US municipal leader and a signal of how support for Palestinian rights remains a signature policy for him. His insistence that the US government arrest Netanyahu has been met with condemnation and ridicule from pro-Israel officials. Pro-Palestinian advocates’ reactions were more mixed – some praised the mayor’s Tuesday statement for its “moral clarity” while others expressed disappointment that he walked back on his promise to seek the Israeli leader’s arrest. “I think the mayor should stay in his lane,” Jonathan Harounoff, Israel’s former international spokesperson at the United Nations, told NY1, accusing him of “political theater”. John Fetterman of Pennsylvania, one of the most staunchly pro-Israel Democrats in the Senate, called Mamdani a “clown” who should “sit down”. “America’s not even part of that corrupt court,” Fetterman said in an interview with Fox News over the weekend. “That’s really not your purview.” Simcha Eichenstein, a pro-Israel New York state assemblymember, had previously called on the state attorney general to review whether Mamdani’s comments about Netanyahu violated state law. The ICC issued arrest warrants against Netanyahu and the former Israeli defense minister Yoav Gallant in 2024, accusing them of war crimes and crimes against humanity, including “starvation as a method of warfare, and … murder, persecution, and other inhumane acts”. “I believe Prime Minister Netanyahu belongs in The Hague,” Mamdani told the New York Times last week, referring to the city in the Netherlands that is home to the court. Neither the US nor Israel are members of the ICC, which the US government has long opposed and which Marco Rubio, the secretary of state, this month threatened to “dismantle” by pressuring other countries to abandon it. Mamdani said he concluded his administration had no authority to execute the ICC arrest warrant, but shifted the burden on the Trump administration to do so. skip past newsletter promotion Free newsletter | Around 2-3 times a day Sign up to Breaking News US Get the most important news as it breaks Preview latest Enter your email Sign up after newsletter promotion “It is clear that we do not have the independent legal authority to enforce this warrant,” he said in Tuesday’s statement. “The federal government, however, does – and I call on them to join the ICC and execute this warrant.” Donald Trump has already said he will do no such thing. Netanyahu “will not be arrested” on US soil, the president said in a social media post earlier this week, adding that the Israeli prime minister was “fighting against the Islamic Republic of Iran” and “the only ones that should be arrested are the people that led Iran into this unprecedented spiral of death and destruction”. Netanyahu had said in the past he was “not concerned” with Mamdani’s threat, and his office said in a statement last week that the mayor “appears interested in diverting public attention from his follies and attacking the leader of the Jewish state and the only democracy in the Middle East”. It called the ICC a “kangaroo court”. Supporters of the court have also praised Mamdani’s efforts to stand up for the tribunal’s mission. “My mayor is doing what any NYC mayor would do if Vladimir Putin or any other indicted war criminal planned to visit NYC,” said Adil Haque, a legal scholar. “He is exploring every legal avenue and exhausting all legal options. If his hands are tied, he will use his voice.”

topic: Trump Deal Saudis Nuclear Delusional Critics

left.title: 'Delusional': Critics explode over Trump deal with Saudis that could set off nuclear race

left-center.title: Trump Administration to Announce Deal That Could Let Saudis Enrich Nuclear Fuel

farLeftText: President Donald Trump spurred terror and outrage Wednesday when reports surfaced that he formally approved a sweeping 30-year nuclear agreement with Saudi Arabia, worth tens of billions of dollars, that could pave the way for uranium enrichment in the Middle East. The accord, expected to be signed Wednesday by Energy Secretary Chris Wright and Saudi Prince Abdulaziz bin Salman, gives American companies a central role in building Saudi Arabia's civilian nuclear infrastructure while locking out foreign competitors like Russia and China, but nonproliferation experts warn the deal could trigger a cascade of nuclear ambitions across the Middle East, reported the Wall Street Journal. "Whither goes Saudi Arabia, so goes the United Arab Emirates, Turkey and Egypt," said Henry Sokolski, executive director of the Nonproliferation Policy Education Center. "The idea that this will have a happy ending is delusional." A key provision would allow U.S. companies to construct a uranium-enrichment facility in Saudi Arabia if a joint two-year study determines the step is warranted, though the facility would operate under a "black box" arrangement designed to keep sensitive enrichment technology out of Saudi hands. Sokolski and other critics say that framework doesn't go far enough, warning that Saudi Arabia's refusal to accept the so-called "gold standard" — a binding commitment never to enrich uranium or reprocess spent fuel domestically — sets a dangerous precedent. Robert Einhorn, a former senior State Department nonproliferation official, echoed those concerns, saying that without adequate constraints on enrichment, the deal "could increase nuclear proliferation risks in the Middle East and beyond." Adding to the alarm is Saudi Arabia's rejection of the International Atomic Energy Agency's "Additional Protocol," which would have subjected the kingdom to more rigorous inspections. Critics argue the alternative monitoring arrangement negotiated by the Trump administration will be insufficient to catch suspected nuclear activity at sites not directly built by the U.S. Congressional critics have also raised objections. Rep. Brad Sherman (D-CA) said lawmakers should withhold approval unless Saudi Arabia agrees to forgo enrichment and reprocessing entirely, arguing the administration shouldn't enable proliferation risks "just because U.S. companies stand to make a profit." The deal also arrives as the White House presses Iran, Saudi Arabia's regional rival, to sharply limit its own nuclear program — a juxtaposition critics call contradictory, even as Trump officials insist the Saudi program will be safeguarded. Blocking the agreement will be difficult because doing so requires a joint congressional resolution capable of overriding a presidential veto, a two-thirds majority threshold rarely cleared on foreign policy disputes. The deal is expected to reach Congress for review in the coming days. Presidents from both parties have long objected to efforts by Middle Eastern countries to enrich uranium, and some lawmakers have argued that a nuclear deal with Saudi Arabia, in particular, was such a sensitive topic that it should face an even tougher congressional standard. A bipartisan group of lawmakers – including now-Secretary of State Marco Rubio – in 2018 sponsored an act that would block nuclear cooperation with the kingdom unless Congress affirmatively voted to approve such a deal, but that measure was never brought to a vote.

topic: Louvre Gallery Reopens Heist Brazen Jewel

left-center.title: Louvre Gallery Reopens After Brazen Jewel Heist

center.title: Watch: Louvre reopens gallery after jewellery heist

farLeftText: No article found in this category for this topic.
centerLeftText: No article found in this category for this topic.
centerText:
The Louvre Museum in Paris reopened its Galerie d'Apollon (Gallery of Apollo) to visitors on Wednesday, nine months after jewellery worth millions was stolen in a brazen heist.

But the gallery, which once housed France's crown jewels, reopened without any on display.

"This is an iconic space within the Louvre, but it will no longer house the Crown Jewels and gemstones, which are to be displayed in secure areas elsewhere in the museum", said the museum's director, Christophe Leribault.

The heist took place on 19 October when masked thieves used a truck with a set of mechanical ladders mounted on it to gain access to the gallery via a balcony.

Police have arrested four people who prosecutors allege are the thieves - but the stolen items have still not been recovered.

topic: Hitlers Birthplace Police Station Austria Nazi

left-center.title: Hitler’s Birthplace in Austria Is Now a Police Station

center.title: Police station opens at Hitler's birthplace in bid to rid site of Nazi link

right.title: Austria Has Converted Hitler's Birthplace Into a Police Station to Deter Nazi Pilgrims

farLeftText: No article found in this category for this topic.
centerLeftText: No article found in this category for this topic.
centerText: Austria has opened a police station in the house where Adolf Hitler was born in the town of Braunau am Inn. It follows years of controversy about what to do with the 17th-Century former inn, where Nazi dictator Adolf Hitler was born in April 1889. The building has now been substantially refurbished and its facade changed, but it has been a huge headache for Austrian authorities, who want to prevent it from becoming a site of neo-Nazi pilgrimage. Hitler only lived in Salzburger Vorstadt 15 for a few weeks before his family moved to another address in Braunau. They left the town for good when he was three years old. Hitler returned briefly to Braunau in 1938, on his way to Vienna, after he annexed Austria to Nazi Germany. The opening ceremony for the police station, featuring police musicians playing a tuba and other brass instruments, was attended by Interior Minister Gerhard Karner and other local dignitaries. Austria was taking "responsibility" for the "terrible legacy" of the past, said Karner. While deciding what to do with the building had not been easy, Karner believed the police station marked "a new chapter" for it. "This house will now become a place of democracy, rule of law, security, freedom and human dignity," he said. A Catholic police chaplain then blessed two crosses which will hang in the new police station. For decades, the interior ministry had rented the building from its former owner, a local woman called Gerlinde Pommer. It paid her a generous rent, in an attempt to prevent the house being used as a site for extreme right-wing tourism.
centerRightText: No article found in this category for this topic.
farRightText: BRAUNAU AM INN, Austria (AP) – Adolf Hitler’s birthplace in Austria was officially inaugurated as a police station on Wednesday in an effort to destroy the building’s appeal as a site of pilgrimage for people who glorify the Nazi dictator. A memorial stone is to remain in place outside the house in Braunau am Inn near the German border with the inscription, “For freedom, democracy and liberty. Never again fascism. Millions of dead remind us.” Hitler was born in the building on April 20, 1889. His family moved to another residence in Braunau am Inn after only a few months, before settling temporarily in Passau in southern Germany. Fueled by racial fanaticism, Hitler’s Nazi regime killed approximately 6 million Jews and other minorities and groups during the Holocaust. The dictator unleashed World War II in which at least 60 million people died. He committed suicide in April 1945, shortly before the end of the war. “The Republic of Austria, the state, the county is facing up to this heritage, the terrible heritage of this time, and commits itself to deal with it,” Austria’s Interior Minister Gerhard Karner said during the opening ceremony of the police station on Wednesday. “This entails this house and this address.” The renovation of the building took several years and cost around 20 million euros ($22.8 million), Austrian news agency APA reported. It was preceded by a yearslong back-and-forth over the ownership of the house. The ownership question was resolved in 2017 when Austria´s highest court ruled the government was within its rights to expropriate the building after its owner refused to sell. A suggestion it might be demolished was dropped. The building had been rented by Austria´s Interior Ministry since 1972 to prevent its misuse and was sublet to various charitable organizations. The structure stood empty after a care center for adults with disabilities moved out in 2011. The Austrian government has said the best use for the building would be the presence of the police as guardians of civil liberties. But critics said the building in its current presentation lacks historical context. A commission of experts came out against turning the building into a museum, advocating instead for a redesign that would not be easily recognizable. The building’s ochre-yellow was turned white, the arched windows were replaced by simple rectangular shapes and the facade has been extended upward toward the roof. Debris from the construction was disposed at a secret location to ensure it would not become a new source for Nazi memorabilia, APA reported. In the past, far-right extremists repeatedly paid homage to the “Führer,” photographing the building, taking stones as souvenirs and laying flowers and wreaths. In 2024, four Germans were caught laying white roses in the building’s window recesses and posing for photos, including a woman giving the stiff-armed Hitler salute.

topic: Mykhailo Drapatyi Ukraines Know General Commanderinchief

left-center.title: Mykhailo Drapatyi: What to Know About Ukraine’s New Top General

center.title: Who is Mykhailo Drapatyi, Ukraine's new commander-in-chief?

farLeftText: No article found in this category for this topic.
centerLeftText:
President Volodymyr Zelensky of Ukraine announced on Tuesday that Maj. Gen. Mykhailo Drapatyi, a decorated fighter, would be the new commander in chief of the country’s armed forces.

The choice of General Drapatyi to replace Gen. Oleksandr Syrskyi followed days of protests over Mr. Zelensky’s decision to fire Ukraine’s defense minister, Mykhailo Fedorov. Mr. Fedorov had clashed with General Syrskyi over warfighting strategy.

Many protesters saw the Fedorov dismissal as a victory for an old guard inside the military that had disagreed with the young, tech-savvy minister’s vision for the future of war.

The appointment of Gen. Drapatyi, 43, is likely to please the protesters who have flooded Ukraine’s streets in recent days, especially since Mr. Drapatyi had publicly sided with Mr. Fedorov.
centerText: Maj Gen Mykhailo Drapatyi, 43, has replaced Gen Oleksandr Syrskyi, 60, as Ukraine's commander-in-chief - the third since the start of Russia's full-scale invasion in 2022. His nomination has been welcomed by both civil society and the military. Drapatyi's name was chanted during protests that started off backing his friend, ex-defence minister Mykhailo Fedorov, but ended up demanding Syrskyi's dismissal. "There's no better person for this position nowadays in Ukraine," one Ukrainian soldier told the BBC. Drapatyi's support rests on years of military experience in post-Soviet Ukraine, his skills as field general and his reputation for being a humane and strong commander among the military. He graduated from the Kharkiv Institute of Tank Troops in 2004 and began serving as a lieutenant. Ten years later he gained prominence when footage went viral showing his infantry fighting vehicle smashing through a makeshift barricade. It was May 2014 and pro-Russian separatists had taken over parts of eastern Ukraine, when Drapatiy's battalion entered the port of Mariupol to free hostages held in the city's police department. He later recalled being told there were barricades more than a metre high ahead and being asked what to do. "I said, 'let's take it by storm.' This video flew around the world," he said in an interview. Later that year, he was commended for withdrawing encircled Ukrainian units from a border area of the Luhansk region. Drapatiy returned to university in 2021 for further operational and strategic training and was presented with a sword of honour as top graduate in June 2021 by British ambassador Melinda Simmons. When Russia's full-scale invasion began in February 2022, Drapatiy was deputy commander of joint forces. He led operational groups - temporary task forces - in several areas of the front line, successfully pushing back the advance of Russian troops towards Kryvyi Rih and liberating a number of settlements in the Kherson and Dnipropretrovsk regions. By 2024 he was heading Ukraine's Ground Forces fighting in some of the east's most intense combat areas. Less than a year later, a training ground in the Dnipropetrovsk region was targeted by a Russian missile and 12 soldiers were killed and 60 others were wounded in the strike. Drapatyi handed in his resignation the very same day, saying he took personal responsibility for the "conspiracy of silence and impunity" that had led to the deaths. "An army in which commanders bear personal responsibility for the lives of personnel keeps living. An army in which nobody is responsible for a loss dies," he wrote. It was seen as a sign of personal accountability, and Zelensky's decision to instead appoint him commander of the Joint Forces of the Armed Forces of Ukraine was widely praised. From then on, whispered rumours of tensions with Syrskyi grew louder. "Drapatyi is a relatively young general. He is gifted. He was always sent to places where the situation was extremely difficult," one unnamed officer told Ukrainian media in June 2025. "I do not think he has political ambitions. Yet I am sure his ambitions are not confined to the position of commander of the ground forces or the Joint Forces." Just over a year later, Syrskyi has gone, and hopes for Drapatyi are high. "Drapatyi has a very good reputation among the rank and file - especially those who joined after 2022 as civilians," says Mykola Bielieskov, senior analyst at the Ukrainian NGO Come Back Alive. But he notes Drapatyi will have to operate under serious constraints. "The commander-in-chief has a lot of leverage. But war is a combination of government, the president's office, military suppliers, dependence on [Western] partners... so he's got a tricky path before him." Prospects of a ceasefire with Russia remain slim and Ukraine faces another harsh winter of war. Mobilising manpower is a priority, and Drapatyi will also be expected to tackle the corruption that touches all levels of the army, from conscription officers to defence procurement. Meanwhile, he will have to capitalise on Ukraine's remarkable technological advances and the burgeoning tech defence industry that has spearheaded Ukraine's recent battlefield successes. In his first message in the new job, Drapatyi acknowledged the difficulty of his task and promised to work "without making loud promises, with responsibility for our decisions, and with respect" for those on the front line. Many will be looking for a change from what was seen as Syrskyi's "outdated" way of fighting, which placed little value on human life in favour of brutal offensives. "It's a major role and people will expect change quickly," analyst Bielieskov says, warning against "oversimplification" that can lead to demand for sweeping improvements in an unrealistic length of time. This has been echoed by former interior ministry adviser Oleksiy Kopytko. As commander-in-chief, he noted, "you are worshipped and loved by all today, not loved by everyone tomorrow, and are guilty of everything the day after tomorrow, with none of your achievements mattering". Valery Zaluzhnyi, who was removed as Ukraine's army chief in 2024, predicted that Drapatyi would "have a very difficult time. Much harder than he imagines". But among many soldiers and ordinary Ukrainians there is a tangible sense of excitement for the new shift in outlook that Drapatyi represents. One said that if Fedorov were reinstated as defence minister, his and Drapatyi's leadership would be "Russia's worst nightmare". When he resigned from his position in 2025, Drapatyi listed issues he had tackled in the Ground Forces - ranging from combat training reform to unfair mobilisation, corruption and flawed procurement systems. "There is expected resistance, but efforts need to be stepped up," he said at the time. He will face similar problems in his new role - albeit on a much larger scale and under the eyes of the whole country. Additional material by BBC Monitoring

topic: Trump Strike Pickaxe Mountain Threatened Suspected

left-center.title: Trump Threatened to Strike Pickaxe Mountain, a Suspected Iranian Nuclear Site. Here’s What to Know.

right.title: Trump: U.S. ‘Not Finished at All’ in Iran; Pickaxe Mountain Strike Coming ‘Pretty Soon’

farLeftText: No article found in this category for this topic.
centerLeftText: No article found in this category for this topic.
centerText: No article found in this category for this topic.
centerRightText: No article found in this category for this topic.
farRightText:
President Donald Trump declared Tuesday that the United States is “not finished at all” with Iran, warning U.S. forces would strike the regime’s heavily fortified Pickaxe Mountain nuclear site “pretty soon” while signaling his administration was prepared to expand military operations rather than return to negotiations.

Trump made the remarks while meeting Lebanese President Joseph Aoun at the White House after being asked about a Wall Street Journal report that Israeli intelligence believes Iran moved thousands of advanced centrifuges into the underground Pickaxe Mountain facility near Natanz last fall.

“I think that they may have,” Trump said when asked about the reported transfer, while stressing that the centrifuges alone would not allow Iran to revive its nuclear program.

“It doesn’t mean anything unless they have the material. They don’t have it. We follow the material. That’s where the action is.”

“We’ll be hitting that area probably pretty soon. There’s not a thing they can do about it,” he said. “Normally, I wouldn’t say that. If I thought they could do something about it, I would never say that. But we’ll be hitting that area pretty soon, and very heavily.”

Pickaxe Mountain, located near Iran’s Natanz nuclear complex, has become an increasing focus of U.S. and Israeli concern because of its extensive underground fortifications. Israeli intelligence reportedly believes Iran transferred advanced centrifuges to the site, while international inspectors have long sought greater access amid concerns the mountain could house undeclared nuclear activities.

Trump suggested any attempt by Tehran to rebuild its nuclear infrastructure would simply invite additional American strikes.

“How about these people? They’re in this because of nuclear weapons, and they’re trying to possibly reconstitute a site? We’ll hit that site,” Trump said. “Any site where they’re even thinking about nuclear, we’ll be hitting it very, very powerfully.”

Trump also made clear the broader U.S. campaign against Iran was far from over, arguing the damage inflicted by American strikes would take Tehran decades to overcome.

“If we left right now, it would take Iran 20 to 25 years to rebuild,” Trump said. “We’re not finished at all… we’re not leaving right now.”

Trump likewise brushed aside suggestions that Washington was preparing to pivot back toward diplomacy, saying Tehran was seeking talks only because of sustained military pressure.

“They want to desperately meet because they’re getting decimated,” Trump said. “Until they’re ready to meet in a meaningful way, we have no interest.”

Trump’s remarks came as U.S. forces continued striking Iranian military infrastructure, with U.S. Central Command announcing additional operations targeting missile and drone capabilities, military command centers, maritime assets, air defense systems, and other facilities tied to Tehran’s efforts to disrupt commercial shipping through the Strait of Hormuz. Trump has repeatedly argued the campaign is intended to prevent Iran from rebuilding its nuclear program while maintaining military pressure on the regime.

Asked about Iran’s remaining capabilities, Trump maintained the administration would continue applying military pressure while monitoring Tehran’s nuclear assets.

“We’re degrading them at levels that nobody thought was possible,” he said, adding that U.S. officials continue to track Iran’s nuclear material rather than focusing solely on centrifuges or infrastructure.

Trump closed with another warning that the administration was prepared to intensify operations if Iran attempted to revive its nuclear program.

“If we left today, it would take them decades to rebuild. I don’t believe they could ever rebuild,” Trump said. “Honestly, they haven’t seen anything yet. We’ve been nice.”

topic: Death Nolan Wells Mississippi Know Independent

left-center.title: What to Know About the Death of Nolan Wells in Mississippi

center.title: Independent autopsy 'inconclusive' on cause of death for 18-year-old Nolan Wells in Mississippi

farLeftText: No article found in this category for this topic.
centerLeftText: No article found in this category for this topic.
centerText:
Independent autopsy 'inconclusive' on cause of death for 18-year-old Nolan Wells in Mississippi

toggle caption Gerald Herbert/AP

An attorney representing the family of Nolan Wells said on Wednesday that an independent autopsy failed to identify a clear cause of death in a case that has drawn national attention. "These findings are inconclusive ... pending further investigation," said Benjamin Crump.

Speaking during a press conference at an NAACP gathering in Chicago, Crump, a prominent civil rights attorney, said the body wasn't in a state that would allow a complete forensic review of the remains. The autopsy was performed by Dr. Roger Mitchell, who was not present.

While Crump acknowledged the autopsy found no evidence of wrongdoing, he described the findings as inconclusive: "This pathologist cannot rule out if any intervening non-accidental factors contributed to or caused the death of Nolan Wells," he said.

Sponsor Message

Wells was found dead on an island off the Mississippi coast two days after he and friends boated to a Fourth of July party.

Wednesday's press conference provided little new concrete information about the case but appeared to be part of an effort to spur faster action from law enforcement.

Wells' mother Christine Wells-Wonsley spoke briefly, voicing anger over the pace and scope of the official investigation. "The only question we have is What happened to our son?" she said. "It is our hope that our local authorities will do what they need to do. Us having the preliminary autopsy results and hearing nothing from them is heartbreaking and concerning."

She added that local law enforcement appeared to conclude prematurely that her son's death was accidental.

"Damage has already been done because that's the narrative that's being pushed by the general public," Wells-Wonsley said.

Law enforcement officials have not yet released an official cause of death, and an official state autopsy is still pending. In a statement released July 8, the Jackson County Sheriff's Office in Mississippi said Wells had last been seen near Horn Island and asked for help from the public as their investigation continues.

"As with any active investigation, our investigators are working to establish the facts through eyewitness accounts, physical evidence, and other reliable information," the statement said.

Sponsor Message

NPR reached out to the Jackson County Sheriff's department following Wednesday's press conference, but have not yet had a response.

During Wednesday's press conference, NAACP President Derrick Johnson accused investigators of mishandling the case. "We are accusing them of being derelict in their duties to follow up and to ensure that, regardless of what happened, there was a proper investigation," he said.

Last week, the NAACP passed a resolution urging law enforcement, including the U.S. Department of Justice, to "complete an in-depth and openly transparent review of how Nolan came to be left on the island and of all activities" leading up to the time when his body was recovered.

At a memorial service on Monday, family members and civil rights leaders called for answers. "For an 18-year-old young man to no longer be with us, we want answers, not bias either way: just answers," Rev. Al Sharpton said during the eulogy. "I think that for the family, the closure can come if there is justice."

Wells was celebrated on Monday as a dearly loved friend, son, brother and teammate. He played football for Ocean Springs High School and Southwest Mississippi Community College.

"Share his stories, and never stop saying his name," said Wells-Wonsley, at the funeral. "As long as we continue to remember him, his light will continue to shine through us all."

Crump announced at a press conference last week that former San Francisco 49ers quarterback Colin Kaepernick would pay for the independent autopsy. Wells was the only Black friend in his group when he went on the Independence Day trip. He's the only member of the group who failed to return.

While investigators haven't confirmed that foul play was involved, the case has drawn widespread attention based in part on concern that race was a factor.

topic: Beluga Whales Canadian Park Dozens Transferred

left-center.title: Dozens of beluga whales transferred to U.S. after Canadian theme park closure

center.title: Watch: Beluga whales travel by plane and lorry to US from closed Canadian marine park

farLeftText: No article found in this category for this topic.
centerLeftText: No article found in this category for this topic.
centerText:
Six beluga whales have arrived at aquariums in the United States after being moved from a closed Canadian marine park that could no longer care for the mammals.

They are part of a large pod of 30 belugas living at Marineland of Canada that need to be rehomed.

Sierra, Acadia, Osiris and Lillooet arrived at a Chicago facility, while Bertie Botts and Frankie arrived at SeaWorld in San Antonio, Texas.

Transported in specialised cold-water containers by truck and plane, the animals were accompanied throughout the journey by marine mammal veterinarians. This is the first stage of an unprecedented international relocation effort.

The move followed months of planning and veterinary checks, with specialist teams now monitoring the whales as they settle into their new homes.
